sessions.title: Freedom
sessions.lastPreview: Sounds good — everything looked fine. I’m here when you need me.

messages.content: which agent are we connected to right now

You’re connected to **Freedom** right now.

This chat’s current session is:
- **Title:** Operator
- **Kind:** operator
awesome thanks
You’re welcome.
okay let's test this again freedom what kind of functionality do you have

Quite a bit.

I can help with things like:

- answer questions and explain things
- plan work, brainstorm, and make decisions with you
- read and edit code in this project
- run terminal commands in the repo
- debug errors, trace bugs, and suggest root-cause fixes
- review architecture and propose cleaner structure
- write docs, changelogs, and summaries
- inspect GitHub PRs/issues if needed
- look things up on the web when current info matters

In this setup specifically, I’m good at being a remote coding/operator copilot — so you can say things like:

- “find why chat scrolling is broken”
- “add rename support for sessions”
- “summarize this file”
- “fix this error”
- “review this PR”
- “make a plan before changing anything”

A few limits:

- I only work inside the current workspace unless you explicitly direct otherwise
- I try to be safe about destructive actions
- for major code changes, I should inspect the flow first rather than just patch symptoms

If you want, I can also give you a **practical menu of commands** — like “things you can ask Freedom to do right now in this repo.”
that's good thank you very much it sounds like we got a bit of a powerful team starting up here

Yeah — I think so too.

You’ve got a pretty strong setup here: you driving, me helping with the heavy lifting, and the desktop/project context tied in. That’s a useful combo.

Whenever you want, we can put it to work on something concrete:
- fix a bug
- improve the mobile UX
- clean up a messy file
- review the current architecture
- plan the next feature

Just point at the next thing and I’ll jump in.
Let's test this voice

Sounds good — voice test received clearly.

I’m here and ready. If you want, try a natural next command like:

- “summarize this project”
- “find the main mobile app flow”
- “show me where session management lives”
- “help me plan the next feature”

Go ahead.
no I meant I was actually testing a voice selection it doesn't seem to be selecting voices and changing it it's still the same voice from the very beginning

I hear you — this is a real bug report: voice selection appears to change in the UI, but playback is still using the original voice.

I’m going to trace the full voice-selection path now: settings/UI → stored state → TTS request → playback. First I’ll do the repo’s required preflight, then inspect the voice flow end to end.
I'm not sure how but it seems to have caught it fine so maybe I have to change the voice re-initialize a new chat maybe or something

Quick check: I’ve already started tracing it. I’m going to look at the exact voice-switch path for a minute so I can tell you whether a new chat should matter or whether that’s a bug.Yeah — a **new chat shouldn’t be required**.

From the path I checked, changing the selected voice is supposed to apply to the **next spoken reply** or the **Test Spoken Reply** action. So:

- it **won’t** change a reply that’s already being spoken
- it **should** affect the next TTS playback
- it **should not** depend on starting a new chat

So your guess about “re-initialize a new chat” sounds more like a **bug/workaround**, not intended behavior.

Best quick test:
1. change the voice
2. tap **Test Spoken Reply**
3. listen for the very next playback

If that still sounds unchanged, then the likely issue is either:
- the phone’s TTS backend is ignoring the selected voice, or
- we need to force a cleaner voice re-apply when the setting changes

If you want, I can keep going and make voice switching more reliable so it’s obvious and immediate.
okay let's double check that because I thought it had let me switch and then it switched back which voice am I listening to you here

I can’t literally hear the voice coming out of your phone from here, so I can’t tell you “you’re hearing Voice X” with certainty.

But I can tell you what the app thinks it should be using, and what that means.
I know that yes please

Yep — the thing to trust here is the app’s **own voice status**, not my guess.

To double-check it:

1. Open **Host**
2. Go to **Voice**
3. Look at **Spoken Reply Voice**
4. Read the line under the voice chips

What it should say:

- if a specific voice is active:  
  **“Current spoken reply voice: [voice name] …”**
- if it fell back:  
  **“Automatic uses the phone’s best available English voice.”**

So in plain English:

- **named voice shown** = that’s the voice it intends to use
- **Automatic shown** = you’re hearing the phone’s default English voice

Then tap **Test Spoken Reply** right after changing it.

If the chip briefly changes and then that helper text goes back to Automatic, or back to a different voice, then yes — it’s switching back and that’s the bug.

If you want, next I can help you do a very exact repro:
- change voice
- test spoken reply
- back out and reopen
- see whether the selected voice persisted or reverted
okay let's run another voice
voice test
hey freedom are you awake and connected
hey freedom I'm back trying to do some testing how's this for the flow
hey freedom hopefully this is back now I'm running another test tell me how this comes back to you
hey Freedom can you hear this report back if you get anything please
hey freedom report back if you get something here on this
hey freedom can you hear me now
Yep — I hear you. What do you want to do?
let's keep that going
Absolutely — I’m here. Go ahead.
okay it's definitely working better now but I'm not hearing anything on my end so I'll get the programmer to check on that

Got it — sounds like input is working better, but return audio still isn’t playing on your side.

That gives the programmer a useful clue: voice capture seems okay, but TTS/output audio is likely where it’s failing.

I’m ready whenever you want to keep testing.
hey Freedom can you hear me now
Yes — I can hear you. Want to test the audio reply again?
okay that's much better because I can hear you too

Perfect — that’s a big improvement. Looks like two-way voice is working now.

Want to keep testing, or move on to something else?
let's keep testing a little bit can you see from the desktop through to the open codex session under the freedom engine OS

I can access the connected workspace and work inside the current Codex session, but I can’t literally see your desktop screen unless you show me a screenshot or point me at something specific.

So: I can inspect the repo, files, and terminal-side state for the Freedom session — just not visually “see” the desktop like screen sharing.

If you want, I can check something specific in the open Codex session now.
I guess I realize it is too literal what is the last point number three that codex sent to me can you read that

If you mean the last numbered “3” I can see from this side, it says:

“Check whether adjacent flows still work.”

If you meant a different Codex message from the desktop chat, I can’t see that directly yet — but you can paste it or point me to it.

no it's not that I'm just more checking the functionality of where you can see cuz the desktop agent I have two instances open and it's not the desktop freedom application I was thinking the vs code one that's probably my mistake

That makes sense — and yes, that distinction matters.

I’m connected to this current Codex workspace/session, not automatically to whatever separate VS Code Codex instance you also have open. So if you have two instances running, I won’t inherently see the other one unless it’s the one tied to this session.

So your test was useful: the connection is working, but scope is session-specific.

Want to keep probing that, or test something else next?
does The voice Loop stop if I open another app over top of the freedom up here

I’m checking the app behavior in the code so I can answer accurately.I found the voice loop code path. I’m checking one more thing: whether the app declares any background audio/voice behavior, because that’s what would decide what happens when another app covers Freedom.Short answer: not by design, but I wouldn’t trust it to keep working fully in the background.

From the code, Freedom keeps a live voice session open and auto-resumes between turns, but I don’t see dedicated background voice handling — more like background updates, not background conversation. So:

- if another app just briefly comes over top, it may keep going
- if Freedom is actually backgrounded, or the other app takes mic/audio focus, the voice loop may stop listening or lose spoken replies

So the practical answer is: **probably not reliably** once another app is over it.

If you want, we can do a real-world test next: start the voice loop, open another app, wait 5 seconds, then come back and see whether Freedom is still listening and still speaking.
okay can you hear me and I've got a chat thing open Above This

Yes — I can hear you.

So at least right now, with that other chat open above this, your voice is still getting through to me. Want to keep pushing it a bit further?
yes I actually know will change gears a bit if I interrupt you while you're talking will you store that and work concurrently on a second thing or is it just going to redirect you

I’m checking the interruption logic so I can answer cleanly.Mostly it redirects me — not true parallel work.

If you interrupt while I’m talking, the system treats that like a barge-in:
- it tries to stop the current spoken reply
- it switches attention to your new input
- then it either sends that new turn next, or briefly holds it until the current run is stopped

So: **interrupt = redirect/replace**, not “keep doing task one while also fully working task two.”

If you want, we can test that right now: start a reply, cut me off midway, and see whether it switches cleanly.
okay well let's try that I'll I don't know just ask you to start answering how well you would handle it if it did distract you

Sure — if you interrupt me while I’m answering, I should stop following the first thread and shift to the new one.

So in practice, if I got distracted mid-answer, the ideal behavior is:
- stop speaking cleanly
- catch your new words
- treat the interruption as the priority
- avoid mixing the two replies together

Go ahead and cut me off whenever you want.
okay I tried to cut you off and it actually didn't even let me cut you off

That’s useful — then barge-in likely isn’t working yet in the live phone flow.

So the current behavior seems to be:
- I speak
- your interruption does not take over immediately
- the system waits for reply audio to finish before really listening again

That points to the interrupt path not winning against active TTS/playback on the phone.

If you want, I can help the programmer narrow it down next — whether it’s:
1. mic not reopening during TTS,
2. interruption threshold too strict,
3. Android audio focus blocking recognition,
4. or the app intentionally pausing listening until speech finishes.
okay I've got the Codex
Codex in vs code working on something a little bit different to
and once it gets interrupted it won't
it won't automatically take the voice and send it

okay Freedom I'm going to test the interrupt and then parallel learning or storage however we want to okay Freedom I'm going to test the interrupt and then parallel learning or storage however you want to call it

Got it — you want to test interrupt behavior first, then parallel learning/storage behavior.

I’m ready. Go ahead with the interrupt test when you want.
okay that didn't interrupt at all

Yep — I found the root cause.

- **Root cause:** the mobile voice loop was keeping speech recognition paused during spoken replies, so barge-in/interrupt could never fire while Freedom was talking.
- **Fix made:** I patched the mobile voice flow to **keep recognition live during TTS** and added an **assistant-echo filter** so Freedom’s own spoken reply won’t self-interrupt.
- **Adjacent checks:** reviewed the mobile interrupt path, voice partial/final transcript handling, TTS playback, and the gateway/desktop stop flow. The backend interrupt path looked okay; the break was on the phone side.
- **Docs/tests:** updated `docs/CHANGELOG.md`; ran mobile **typecheck** and targeted tests for `voiceSessionMachine`, `assistantSpeechRuntime`, and `voiceService` — all passed. No exceptions.

If you want, next step is: **rebuild/reload the mobile app and test interrupt again while Freedom is actively speaking.**
okay I'm going to do a test here again so first of all I'll just double check with a straight up reply
Sounds good — go ahead with the straight reply test first, and then we can try interrupt again right after.
interrupt again right after next one give me a couple of decent sentences and I'll try the interrupt thing

Absolutely. I’m going to give you a normal reply with enough length to make the interrupt test meaningful. After this starts speaking, jump in whenever you want and we’ll see whether the barge-in path actually cuts me off this time.

If it works, we should see the reply stop quickly and your new turn take over instead of waiting for the speech to finish.
okay hold on there for a second I wanted to see so now that I've interrupted so far that works let's see if you get this and still remember the previous discussion

Yep — I got that, and I still remember the thread.

We were testing:
- **interrupt/barge-in first**
- then **parallel learning/storage** after that

And this latest test suggests the interrupt path is now working.

If you want, we can move straight into the **parallel learning/storage** test next.
if you want we can move straight into

Yep — let’s move straight into the **parallel learning/storage** test.

Give me the test prompt you want to use, and I’ll track it as a continuation of this thread.

yep let's move straight into the second actually this is a little weird you your voice on the TTS got picked up by the microphone and essentially it just took part of your sentence and moved it in as an interrupt

Yeah — that’s a real bug, and it’s a useful result.

What happened is:

- **the interrupt path is working**
- but **my own TTS audio got re-captured by the mic**
- so part of my sentence was treated like a new interrupt

So this is no longer “interrupt doesn’t work.”
It’s now **echo/self-interrupt filtering isn’t strong enough yet**.

If you want, I can fix that next by tightening the **assistant-voice echo rejection** so Freedom ignores its own spoken reply while still allowing a real user barge-in.
what happened is the interrupt path okay I'm going to get the Codex on vs code to try to fix that first do one more push and then I got to get to bed

Makes sense.

Here’s the clean handoff for VS Code:

- **Good news:** real interrupt/barge-in now works.
- **Current bug:** Freedom’s **own TTS audio is being re-captured by the mic** and treated as a user interrupt.
- **So the next fix is:** stronger **self-echo / assistant-speech rejection** without breaking genuine user barge-in.

For one last push, I’d frame it as:

> Keep live barge-in enabled, but ignore transcript that strongly matches the currently spoken assistant chunk/draft, especially immediately after TTS starts.

Then get some sleep. You’ve already narrowed it down well.
thanks good night
Good night. We made solid progress.
okay one more quick track on the adjustments on the echo see if that does not interrupt yourself

Yep — let’s do one quick echo test.

I’m going to give you a normal spoken reply now, and the goal is that **I should keep talking without cutting myself off**. If this still self-interrupts, then the echo filter still needs tightening.
if they still self interrupts then the echo feels

Right — if it still self-interrupts, the echo filter is still too loose.

That would mean:
- real barge-in is working
- but self-echo rejection still needs tightening a bit more

If you want, give me one short final result:
- **worked**
- or **still self-interrupted**
right if it's still self interrupts the

Exactly — if it still self-interrupts, then the fix is only partial.

That means:
- **user interrupt:** working
- **self-echo protection:** still not strong enough

Give me the result in one phrase:
- **worked**
- **still self-interrupted**
exactly if it's still selfish okay stop it's it's self-interrupting for sure

Got it — confirmed: **real interrupt works, but self-interrupt is still happening**.

So the remaining bug is:
- TTS audio is still being picked up by the mic
- and it’s still passing the interrupt check

Best next fix:
- compare against the **currently spoken chunk**, not just the full draft
- add a short **do-not-interrupt guard window** right after TTS starts
- require a stronger mismatch before treating speech as a real barge-in

That’s a clean stopping point. We’ve narrowed it down well.
I want to run another quick test of the voice here please just read something back I'm trying to pick what voice to use

Absolutely — here’s a clean sample:

This is a voice test for Freedom. I’m speaking in a steady, natural way so you can judge the tone, clarity, and pacing. If this voice feels easy to listen to, it’s probably a good candidate for regular use.
hey let's do a voice test here while I'm trying to pick please

Sure — here’s a fresh voice test:

Hey Adam, this is Freedom doing a short voice check. I’m speaking clearly and evenly so you can listen for tone, warmth, and how natural the pacing feels. If this voice sounds easy to follow, it’s probably a strong choice.
okay let's try another voice test please

Of course — here’s another one:

This is another quick voice sample from Freedom. I’m aiming for a calm, clear tone with a little warmth, so you can hear whether it feels natural enough for longer conversations. If this one feels smoother or more pleasant, it may be the better pick.
try another voice test here please

Sure — here’s another sample:

Hi Adam, this is Freedom with one more voice check. I’m keeping the pace relaxed and the wording simple so you can focus on the sound of the voice itself. Listen for clarity, comfort, and whether it feels good for everyday back-and-forth.
and another voice test please

Absolutely — one more:

This is Freedom with another quick voice sample. I’m speaking in a steady, conversational way so you can judge whether the tone feels clear, calm, and natural over a longer session. If this one feels easy to listen to, it may be the right choice.
and another one please

Sure — here’s another:

Hey Adam, this is one more short voice check from Freedom. I’m keeping the delivery warm, steady, and easygoing so you can notice whether it feels pleasant and natural enough to hear often. Sometimes the best choice is just the one that feels least tiring.
they all sound exactly the same even after I select a different one why are they not refreshing

I found the likely cause.

It wasn’t really “refreshing” because:
- the app was **updating the selected voice in UI/settings**
- but the phone TTS backend could stay **warm on the same voice**
- and we were **trusting the requested voice**, not the one Android actually applied

So it could look like the voice changed while the phone kept speaking with the same default voice.

I’ve patched it so:
- switching voices now **forces a TTS backend refresh**
- and the app will **warn you if Android doesn’t actually confirm the new voice**

I also ran mobile **typecheck** and the **TTS tests** after the change.

So the short answer is: **the picker state was changing, but the actual TTS engine wasn’t reliably reapplying the new voice.**
I've patched it so switching voices now forces at TTS backhand

Yep — that’s the gist.

A cleaner way to say it:

**I patched it so switching voices now forces a TTS backend refresh, instead of just updating the selected voice in the UI.**

And also:

**If Android doesn’t actually apply the requested voice, the app should now warn about that instead of pretending the switch worked.**
yeah oh yeah instead of just updating the selected

Exactly —

**instead of just updating the selected voice in the UI, it now tries to reinitialize the actual phone TTS backend so the new voice really takes effect.**
okay I'm going to go select a different voice and see if there's a difference

Perfect — try a clearly different one, then run **Test Spoken Reply**.

If it still sounds the same, tell me whether:
- the UI showed the new voice as selected
- and whether you got any warning that Android didn’t confirm the switch
okay I just switched it let's run the test please

Absolutely — here’s the test sample:

This is Freedom running a fresh spoken reply check after the voice change. I’m speaking clearly and at an even pace so you can listen for whether the tone, accent, or overall character actually sounds different now.
no it's it's the same voice again
it's definitely the same voice
I’m going to do the required preflight, then trace the voice-selection path end to end.
okay one quick voice test here

messages.errorMessage: Recovered from a stale stop request.

messages.content: hey freedom I want to do a quick voice check here is everything coming through

Yep — you’re coming through clearly.

I got: “hey freedom I want to do a quick voice check here is everything coming through”

Want to do one more test, like a longer sentence or a punctuation/code-word check?
no that's okay I'm good for now thanks
Sounds good — everything looked fine. I’m here when you need me.
